scenario.title: Autonomous Emergency Brake
scenario.narrative: A self-driving car crashed. The brake decision combines FOUR conditions, producing a 2^4 = 16-row truth table. Full BCC would demand all 16 crash-test logs, but the Judge has restricted your subpoena budget to just 3. Pick any 3 logs to introduce into evidence — the lesson is to feel how much of the combinatorial space is left untouched.
scenario.code:
if obstacle_ahead and speed_gt_50 and not_wet and no_manual_brake:
    trigger_emergency_brake()

conditions.label: Driver did not press the manual brake

claim: BCC demands all 16 combinations, but the court budget covers only 3 subpoenas. Pick exactly 3 logs and prove to the jury how much of the truth table you are forced to leave dark.
charges: The decision under test has 4 conditions — that is 2^4 = 16 distinct combinations under BCC.
The court budget allows only 3 logs to enter evidence. Pick any 3 from the 16-row table.
The point of this exercise is not which 3 you pick — it is to see that 13 combinations remain completely untested. This is the combinatorial explosion problem BCC suffers from at scale.
correct_answer_explanation: Verdict: 3 of 16 combinations subpoenaed — 13 untested! With only 4 conditions you already need 16 BCC tests; double the conditions to 8 and you would need 256; reach 10 conditions and BCC explodes to 1,024 tests. BCC is mathematically perfect but practically infeasible for any non-trivial decision. This is precisely the gap that MC/DC fills in the next chapter: linear N+1 growth with strong fault-detection guarantees, instead of exponential 2^N.
wrong_answer_explanation: Pick exactly 3 rows — the budget is hard-capped. The lesson is the size of the gap, not which specific rows you pick.
hints: Court Budget: you can only subpoena 3 of the 16 rows. There is no single 'right' triplet — every choice leaves 13 rows untested.
Tip: pick the all-TRUE row (R1, the only outcome=TRUE case) plus two single-flip neighbours so the gap feels intuitive. But it is your call.

Worked solution: any 3 rows are accepted. The key takeaway is that BCC at 4 conditions already needs 16 logs, and at 10 conditions it explodes to 1,024. This is why the next chapter introduces MC/DC, which scales as N+1 instead of 2^N.

misconceptions.explanation_md: As the number of conditions N grows, BCC suffers from **combinatorial explosion**: the suite size doubles with every new condition (2^N). For N=10 that is already 1,024 tests, and for N=20 it is over a million. BCC is the mathematical gold standard for combination coverage, but it is rarely feasible in practice. MC/DC was introduced precisely to retain strong fault-detection guarantees while keeping the suite size linear (N+1).
